entities.description: Hermann Varnhagen's Royal MS 18 A X transcription, with German introduction, textual notes, and comparison of other witnesses, in the first section of his Anglia article.
entities.label: Varnhagen's 1879 edition of the Royal body-and-soul debate
notes: The article's first section, 'Zu den Streitgedichten zwischen Körper und Seele,' runs from p. 225 into p. 252. The Royal transcription represented by CME00101 occupies pp. 229-244.
The German introduction begins on p. 225 and continues onto p. 229; editorial notes begin immediately after the Royal explicit on p. 244. Neither surrounding portion is encoded in CME00101.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1879, and Internet Archive metadata for the scanned volume states NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT with no visible copyright notice and the stated 1879 date.
rights.component: Varnhagen's 1879 text, editorial introduction, and notes

entities.label: London, British Library, Royal MS 18 A X
notes: The current British Library catalogue's Digitised Content field is blank. This is recorded as a dated access finding, not as proof that no digitization can ever exist.

The work occupies ff. 61v-66v; the next catalogued item begins on f. 67.
Stanza IV, corresponding to lines 25-32, was added by another hand on a small inserted vellum strip foliated 62.
The poem has 67 eight-line stanzas, with two verse lines written per manuscript line according to the British Library description.

reviewNotes: The source chain distinguishes the repository artifact, U-M digital encoding, 1879 scholarly edition, medieval manuscript witnesses, printed-volume facsimile, and DIMEV catalogue dataset.

DIMEV v1.0 calls record 605 French/macaronic and gives a malformed Royal explicit. Those fields conflict with the direct Royal transcription and current British Library catalogue and are documented rather than propagated.
The cached Anglia PDF is a complete scan of the printed volume, not a manuscript facsimile.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a version of the University of Michigan CME00101 electronic text, encoded from Hermann Varnhagen's 1879 transcription on printed pp. 229-244. Varnhagen transcribed British Library Royal MS 18 A X, ff. 61v-66v, and collated other witnesses in apparatus excluded from the XML. A complete Internet Archive scan of Anglia volume 2 and the exact DIMEV v1.0 dataset are cached with fixity; no complete public facsimile of the Royal manuscript was found as of 2026-07-13.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Composed by the early fourteenth century at the latest; first attested in a late-thirteenth- or early-fourteenth-century witness
catalogSummary.region: England; earliest known witness associated with the West Midlands
catalogSummary.title: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule

catalogingSubject.label: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule
catalogingSubject.scope_note: One anonymous Middle English body-and-soul debate poem, beginning 'As i lay in a wynternyght'. The eight nested DIV2 elements in CME00101 are dialogue or scene sections within the poem, not eight additional works.

contentParts.label: Opening vision and the soul's first accusation
contentParts.summary: The narrator sees a corpse at night; the soul addresses the body and blames its worldly appetites for their damnation.

contentParts.label: Respondit corpus ad animam
contentParts.summary: The body answers that the soul, endowed with reason, should have ruled it and is therefore culpable.

contentParts.label: Anima dicit ad corpus
contentParts.summary: The soul renews its accusation, dwelling on the body's vanity, luxury, and present corruption.

contentParts.label: Ait corpus ad animam
contentParts.summary: The body again charges the soul with failing to restrain bodily desire while both still had time to repent.

contentParts.label: Anima dicit ad corpus
contentParts.summary: The soul contrasts their former comforts with the body's grave and anticipates infernal punishment.

contentParts.label: Corpus ait ad animam
contentParts.summary: The body laments its decay and the separation from the soul.

contentParts.label: Anima dicit ad corpus
contentParts.summary: The soul rejects the body's excuses and reviews the moral failures that brought them to judgment.

contentParts.label: Demonibus venientibus ad animam
notes: This section comprises ten eight-line stanzas in the source, but one XML LG spans encoded lines 433-448 and therefore yields nine LG elements.
contentParts.summary: Devils arrive, mock the soul, enumerate its sins, and seize it for punishment.

contentParts.label: Iterum de anima dicente et clamante ad salvatorem suum
contentParts.summary: The soul cries to Christ; the narrator closes with an admonition to remember death and seek mercy.

assertion.notes: The late-thirteenth- or early-fourteenth-century Laud witness establishes circulation by this outer bound, but no authorial copy or exact composition date survives.

dateStatements.display: By the early fourteenth century at the latest

assertion.notes: This is the earliest currently identified witness among DIMEV's seven witnesses, not a composition date.

dateStatements.display: Late thirteenth or early fourteenth century (Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108)

extent.description: One poem of 536 verse lines in nine intellectual dialogue or scene sections. The XML has one DIV1, eight nested DIV2 elements, 66 LG elements, 536 L elements, and 16 PB elements. Varnhagen and the British Library catalogue count 67 eight-line stanzas; XML lines 433-448 are held in one 16-line LG and therefore reduce the encoded LG count by one.

formStatements.description: A 536-line stanzaic poem: 67 eight-line stanzas in the edition and manuscript catalogue, represented by 66 LG elements in the XML because one LG spans two stanzas.

assertion.notes: The narrator's nocturnal visionary frame introduces the dialogue.

Short Latin speech labels and the explicit are embedded paratext and are not promoted to a whole-work language facet. DIMEV v1.0's record-level French/macaronic label reflects a French benediction unique to the collateral Laud witness and does not describe the Royal text encoded here.

notes: The Royal manuscript's holding in London and Varnhagen's publication in Halle are lineage facts, not composition places.
DIMEV's seven entries are manuscript witnesses of this work. The Latin Visio Philiberti and other vernacular body-and-soul debates are modeled separately as related works.
The XML's 66 LG elements should not be silently normalized to 67: it is an encoding fact caused by one 16-line LG, while 67 stanzas is the textual and manuscript-catalogue count.

next_steps: Consult a current critical edition or dedicated stemmatic study of all seven witnesses.
Keep witness dialect and production-place evidence scoped to each manuscript rather than transferring it to the work.
notes: The date and region currently reported are bounded, evidence-backed cataloging statements, not claims of exact composition circumstances.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's composition be dated or localized more narrowly than the early-fourteenth-century outer bound in England?

assertion.notes: The English-language work is transmitted in English manuscript witnesses; this broad cultural origin does not localize its composition more narrowly.
The place statement describes the witness's reported production region. It is not evidence that the poem was composed there.
No named Middle English author is supported. Historical attributions attached to the Latin Visio Philiberti are not transferred to this English adaptation.

scope.description: The Royal MS 18 A X transcription and apparatus published in Anglia 2 (1879), pp. 225-255
The Royal MS 18 A X text printed on pp. 229-244 and represented by CME00101
Variant comparison of the Royal text with the Laud, Auchinleck, Vernon, and Digby witnesses in the article's notes
German introduction and textual notes in the 1879 article; these passages are not included in CME00101

summaries.value: A dreamer overhears a dead person's soul and body accuse one another of responsibility for sin. Their alternating speeches move from mutual reproach and the body's lament to the arrival of devils, the soul's appeal to Christ, and a warning to the living about death, judgment, and repentance.

CME00101 contains Varnhagen's Royal MS 18 A X transcription from its heading through 'Amen' and the explicit, printed on pp. 229-244. It excludes the article's German introduction on pp. 225-229 and the editorial notes and comparison with other witnesses that begin after the explicit on p. 244.

DIMEV 605 identifies seven manuscript witnesses of this Middle English work. They are witnesses or versions of the same poem, not seven independent related works. The Royal witness represented here has 67 eight-line stanzas; stanza IV was supplied in another hand on an inserted vellum strip now foliated 62.

assertion.notes: A cataloging title based on established Middle English forms; it is not asserted as an authorial title.

titles.value: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule

scope.description: Rubric in Royal MS 18 A X and heading of Varnhagen's transcription

titles.value: A disputeson betwen the body and the sowle

notes: DIMEV regularizes the incipit as 'As I lay in a winters night'; other witnesses and scholarship often supply the short designation 'Als I lay'.

scope.description: Opening words in the Royal-witness transcription

titles.value: As i lay in a wynternyght

assertion.notes: The DIMEV v1.0 Royal-witness field gives a conflicting malformed explicit; the direct edition and current British Library catalogue agree on this reading.

scope.description: Latin colophon in Royal MS 18 A X

titles.value: Explicit disputacio inter corpus et animam

work.author: Anonymous
work.dateDisplay: Composed by the early fourteenth century at the latest; first attested in a late-thirteenth- or early-fourteenth-century witness
work.displayTitle: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule
work.preferredTitle: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule

publication.embeddedTitle: A disputeson betwen the body and the sowle.

work.regionDisplay: England; earliest known witness associated with the West Midlands
work.summary: A dreamer overhears a dead person's soul and body accuse one another of responsibility for sin. Their alternating speeches move from mutual reproach and the body's lament to the arrival of devils, the soul's appeal to Christ, and a warning to the living about death, judgment, and repentance.
